description: Generic analytics dashboard for real applications. Features dynamic injection of application-specific panels at Layer 4+.

panels.title: Request Latency — p50 / p95 / p99
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Time from when the service receives a request to when it sends the response, expressed as percentile distributions.

**p50 (median):** Half of requests complete faster than this. Represents the typical user experience.

**p95:** 1 in 20 requests is slower than this. A good SLA target for most APIs.

**p99:** 1 in 100 requests is slower than this. Reveals tail latency that averages hide — the experience of your worst-affected users.

**How collected:** Prometheus Histogram metric (`http_request_duration_seconds`). The application records the duration of every request. PromQL `histogram_quantile()` computes percentiles from the bucket counts.

**Thresholds:** Warn p99 > 1s · Critical p99 > 3s. Tighten for payment or auth flows (Warn > 500ms).

**Common causes of spikes:** Slow database queries, GC pauses, downstream dependency latency, thread pool saturation.

panels.title: Traffic — Requests per Second
panels.description:
**What it measures:** The volume of requests your application is processing per second, broken down by HTTP status class.

**Why it matters:** Traffic is your baseline. All other metrics must be interpreted relative to it. A 1% error rate means very different things at 10 RPS vs 10,000 RPS.

**How collected:** Prometheus Counter (`http_requests_total`) incremented on every request. PromQL `rate()` converts the cumulative counter into a per-second rate over a sliding window.

**What to watch:** Sudden drops (traffic fell away — routing issue?) are as important as spikes. Compare to same time yesterday using `offset 1d` in PromQL.

**Thresholds:** Alert on deviation > 3× from rolling baseline rather than a fixed number.

panels.title: Error Count — 4xx / 5xx (per scrape interval)
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Number of error responses in each scrape interval.

Uses `increase()` over `$__rate_interval` so every data point shows the actual count of errors in that window — not a per-second rate.

**Click any data point to open the Error Drilldown table** (per-endpoint breakdown).

panels.title: Saturation — DB Connections / CPU / Memory
panels.description:
**Resource saturation for the three most common bottlenecks.**

All values are normalised to 0–100% so they share the same axis:

- **DB Connections:** `active_connections / max_connections` per app. Pool exhaustion causes 'too many connections' errors.
- **CPU:** Average CPU utilisation across all cores on the host (from `node_exporter`).
- **Memory:** Used RAM as a % of total (`MemAvailable` method, includes reclaimable cache).

**Thresholds (shared):** Warn > 70% · Critical > 90%.

panels.title: CPU Utilization %
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Percentage of CPU time spent on non-idle work per host, broken down by mode: user (application code), system (kernel), iowait (waiting for disk I/O), steal (stolen by hypervisor on VMs).

**How collected:** `node_exporter` reads `/proc/stat` and exposes `node_cpu_seconds_total` counters per mode per core. PromQL computes the rate of non-idle time.

**Thresholds:** Warn > 70% · Critical > 90% sustained for 5 minutes.

**Key modes to watch:** High `iowait` means disk is the bottleneck. High `steal` means the VM host is overloaded — consider migrating to a less noisy host.

panels.title: Memory — Used vs Available
panels.description:
**What it measures:** RAM used by applications and the kernel vs RAM available for new allocations. Uses `MemAvailable` (not `MemFree`) which includes reclaimable page cache.

**How collected:** `node_exporter` reads `/proc/meminfo` and exposes `node_memory_*` gauges.

**Why MemAvailable matters:** Linux aggressively uses free RAM for file caching (beneficial). `MemFree` appears low even on healthy systems. `MemAvailable` is what the kernel can actually give to a new process.

**Thresholds:** Warn when MemAvailable < 20% of total · Critical < 10%.

**Watch for:** Any swap usage is a critical signal — apps hitting swap experience 100–1000× memory access latency.

panels.title: Swap Usage (should be 0)
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Amount of swap space (disk used as overflow memory) currently in use. Should always be zero on a healthy system.

**Why it matters:** When a system uses swap, it is treating disk as RAM. Disk is 100–1000× slower than RAM. Any process reading from swap will experience severe latency spikes.

**How collected:** `node_exporter` exposes `node_memory_SwapTotal_bytes` and `node_memory_SwapFree_bytes`. Used = Total − Free.

**Thresholds:** Any nonzero value is Critical — treat as a P1 incident. The OOM killer is the alternative, which terminates processes.

**Common causes:** Memory leak, under-provisioned host, JVM heap set too large relative to available RAM.

panels.title: Disk I/O Throughput
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Bytes read from and written to disk per second per device.

**How collected:** `node_exporter` reads `/proc/diskstats` and exposes `node_disk_read_bytes_total` and `node_disk_written_bytes_total` counters.

**Saturation metric:** `rate(node_disk_io_time_seconds_total[5m])` approaching 1.0 means the disk is 100% busy and I/O operations are queuing.

**Thresholds:** Disk utilisation Warn > 70% · Critical > 90%. Write latency Warn > 10ms (SSD) · Warn > 50ms (spinning disk).

**Common causes of high write I/O:** Database writes, application logging, backup jobs, log rotation.

panels.title: Disk Space Used %
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Percentage of disk space used per filesystem mount point.

**How collected:** `node_exporter` reads filesystem stats via `statfs()` and exposes `node_filesystem_avail_bytes` and `node_filesystem_size_bytes`.

**Thresholds:** Warn < 20% free · Critical < 10% free.

**Best practice:** Alert on rate of fill, not just current percentage. A disk filling at 1GB/hour needs a different response than one filling at 1MB/hour. Use `predict_linear(node_filesystem_avail_bytes[6h], 4*3600) < 0` to alert when a disk will fill within 4 hours.

panels.title: Network Throughput RX / TX
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Bytes received (RX) and transmitted (TX) per network interface per second.

**How collected:** `node_exporter` reads `/proc/net/dev` and exposes `node_network_receive_bytes_total` and `node_network_transmit_bytes_total` counters per interface.

**Thresholds:** Compare against your interface speed (1Gbps = 125MB/s). Alert when sustained throughput > 80% of interface capacity.

**Also monitor:** `node_network_receive_drop_total` and `node_network_transmit_drop_total` — any packet drops indicate saturation or hardware issues.

panels.title: Request Rate by Endpoint
panels.description:
**What it measures:** HTTP requests per second broken down by individual route/endpoint.

**Why it matters:** Aggregate traffic hides the distribution. 1,000 RPS could mean 990 on `/health` and 10 on `/checkout` — very different implications for scaling.

**How collected:** `http_requests_total` counter with a `route` label set to the route template (e.g. `/api/orders/{id}` not `/api/orders/12345`). PromQL `sum by(route)` groups by endpoint.

**Drill-down:** Click a spike on this chart to correlate with the latency panel — filter by the same route label to see whether the high-traffic endpoint is also slow.

**Warning:** Never use user IDs or raw URLs as label values — this creates unbounded label cardinality that will crash Prometheus.

panels.title: Error Count by Status Code (per scrape interval)
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Count of error responses by HTTP status code in each interval.

Uses `increase()` to show the actual number of errors rather than a rate.

**Click any line to drill into the Error Endpoint Drilldown table.**

panels.title: Business Transactions Rate (logins)
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Domain-specific event counts — transactions completed, logins, report generations, or any meaningful business action — expressed as a rate per second.

**Why it matters:** A system can be technically healthy (zero 5xx, low latency) and still fail to deliver business value. Business transaction metrics catch silent failures — for example, a payment form that always returns 200 OK but never actually processes the payment.

**How collected:** Explicit `Counter` increments at each business event in application code. Named after the action: `business_transactions_total{type='transaction_completed'}`.

**Alert pattern:** Alert on percentage drops from rolling baseline (`< baseline * 0.5`) rather than fixed numbers. A drop in transaction rate is often the first symptom of a broken user journey.

panels.title: External Dependency Latency p95
panels.description:
**What it measures:** The 95th percentile latency of outbound HTTP calls from your application to downstream services — databases exposed via HTTP, external APIs, microservices, etc.

**Why it matters:** Your application's latency cannot be better than the sum of its slowest dependency calls. If a downstream service degrades, your latency and error rate will follow within seconds.

**How collected:** `http_client_request_duration_seconds` Histogram with an `upstream` label identifying the downstream service. PromQL `histogram_quantile(0.95)` per upstream.

**What to watch:** Each upstream line independently. If one upstream's latency climbs while others stay flat, the problem is in that specific dependency, not your application.

**Thresholds:** Warn when any upstream p95 > 2× its historical baseline.

panels.title: DB Query Latency (p95 / p99)
panels.description:
**What it measures:** How long the database takes to execute queries, at the 95th and 99th percentile. Includes parse time, planning time, lock wait time, and execution time.

**How collected:** PostgreSQL: `pg_stat_statements` extension + `postgres_exporter`. MySQL: `mysqld_exporter` with slow query log. The exporter exposes query timing as a Histogram.

**Why p99 matters:** A query that is fast 99% of the time but takes 10 seconds 1% of the time will devastate tail latency for your users.

**Thresholds:** OLTP Warn p99 > 100ms · Critical p99 > 500ms.

**Common causes of latency spikes:** Missing index, stale statistics causing plan regression, lock contention, replication lag on read replicas, vacuum running on a large table.

panels.title: DB Active Connections %
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Active database connections as a percentage of the maximum allowed (`max_connections` in PostgreSQL).

**Why it matters:** Approaching `max_connections` causes 'too many connections' errors that make the application completely unable to reach the database.

**How collected:** `postgres_exporter` queries `pg_stat_activity` for active connection count and `pg_settings` for `max_connections`. The ratio is the saturation metric.

**Thresholds:** Warn > 70% · Critical > 90%.

**Best practice:** Use a connection pooler (PgBouncer) in front of PostgreSQL. This allows many application threads to share a small number of actual database connections, preventing exhaustion under load.

panels.title: Replication Lag
panels.description:
**What it measures:** The delay between a write being committed on the primary database and that write being applied on replica databases, in seconds.

**Why it matters:** Applications reading from replicas see stale data during lag. More critically, if the primary fails during high replication lag, the data written during that window is lost in a failover.

**How collected:** `postgres_exporter` queries `pg_stat_replication` on the primary for bytes of WAL not yet replicated. MySQL: `mysqld_exporter` reads `Seconds_Behind_Master` from replica status.

**Thresholds:** Warn > 10s · Critical > 60s. For financial data: Warn > 2s.

**Common causes:** Network issues between primary and replica, replica under-resourced, very high write throughput on primary.

panels.title: Lock Waits & Deadlocks
panels.description:
**What it measures:** The number of database lock waits and deadlocks over time. Lock waits occur when a transaction needs a lock held by another transaction.

**Why it matters:** Lock contention degrades throughput non-linearly — a few long-running transactions can block hundreds of others. Deadlocks cause transaction rollbacks and application errors.

**How collected:** PostgreSQL: `pg_locks` view counts locks by type. MySQL: `Innodb_deadlocks` status variable via `mysqld_exporter`.

**Thresholds:** Any increase in deadlocks should be investigated. Lock waits > 5 concurrent is Warn.

**Common causes:** Long-running transactions, missing indexes causing full table scans that hold locks, application not committing transactions promptly.

panels.title: Database Size Growth
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Total size of each database in bytes, tracked over time to show growth trends.

**How collected:** `postgres_exporter` queries `pg_database_size()` function which returns size in bytes per database.

**Why it matters:** Unexpected growth spikes can indicate a runaway job inserting data, missing delete/archival processes, or a data leak. Predictable growth helps capacity planning.

**Best practice:** Combine with tablespace-level breakdown to identify which tables are growing fastest. Alert when growth rate exceeds 3× the 30-day average daily growth.

**Also monitor:** Table bloat from unvacuumed dead tuples in PostgreSQL — the table file grows even without new data.

panels.title: 📊 Performance Drilldown — Slowest Endpoints
panels.description:
**Per-endpoint performance breakdown.** Each row is one route/operation. Duration (avg) is the mean response time for that endpoint over the selected time window. Count is total requests. Sorted slowest-first.

Click an endpoint name to view the granular Operation Detail panel.

panels.title: 🚨 Error Drilldown — Endpoints with Highest Error Count
panels.description:
**Per-endpoint error breakdown.** Sorted by highest error count.

- **Operation (Endpoint):** Route template.
- **Error Count:** Total 4xx + 5xx responses in the time window.
- **Avg Duration:** Mean response time (includes error responses).

Click an endpoint to open the granular Operation Detail view.

panels.title: 🔍 Operation Detail — ${route:raw}
panels.description:
Granular view for the selected endpoint.

Shows error count, total request rate and p95 latency for this single operation. Use the time range picker to zoom into the incident window.

**For deeper log investigation** connect Grafana Loki or Elasticsearch and filter by `app=$app route=$route status_code=~[45]..`

panels.title: Authentication Attempts — Success vs Failure
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Rate of authentication attempts (user login / token exchange) split by success/failure.

This is the front door of AuthApi. A spike in failures could indicate brute-force attempts, credential issues, or Cognito outages.

panels.title: Token Validations — Valid vs Invalid
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Rate of JWT token validation calls, split by valid/invalid.

Every downstream service call to `/api/auth/validate` is counted here. Invalid tokens may indicate expired sessions, tampered tokens, or clock skew.

panels.title: Auth Failure Breakdown (by operation & reason)
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Auth failures broken down by operation (token, validate) and specific failure reason.

This panel shows *why* authentication is failing — invalic credentials, expired tokens, Cognito errors, etc. Requires the `auth_failures_total` counter to have data.

panels.title: Token Validation Failures (by reason)
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Why token validations are failing — expired, malformed, invalid signature, wrong audience, etc.

Requires the `auth_token_validation_failures_total` counter to have data.

panels.title: Latency per Route — p50 / p95
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Request latency broken down per API route.

`/api/auth/token` is expected to be slower (involves Cognito SDK calls + OAuth2 token exchange).
`/api/auth/validate` should be fast (local JWKS key lookup + JWT verification).

panels.title: Dependency Request Rate
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Rate of outbound calls to external dependencies.

**cognito / DescribeUserPool:** Called during token exchange to validate the user pool.
**cognito / oauth2_token:** The actual OAuth2 token request to Cognito.
**jwks / getSigningKey:** JWKS key fetch for JWT signature verification.

panels.title: Dependency Latency — p50 / p95
panels.description:
**What it measures:** How long each dependency call takes.

Cognito SDK calls are typically 100-500ms. JWKS key lookups should be <50ms (cached). A spike here directly impacts AuthApi response times.

panels.title: Dependency Failures
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Rate of failed calls to external dependencies.

Any non-zero value here is concerning. Cognito failures = users can't authenticate. JWKS failures = tokens can't be validated.

panels.title: Dependency Success Rate
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Success ratio for each external dependency.

Targets: >99% for all dependencies. Below 95% is critical — the auth service is effectively degraded.

panels.title: Heap Memory — Used / Total / External
panels.description:
**What it measures:** V8 heap memory allocation.

**Heap Used:** Active JS objects. Watch for monotonic growth (memory leak).
**Heap Total:** Memory allocated by V8. Grows in chunks.
**External:** C++ objects bound to JS (Buffers, etc.).

If Heap Used approaches Heap Total consistently, the GC is under pressure.

panels.title: Event Loop Lag — p50 / p99 / Current
panels.description:
**What it measures:** How long callbacks wait before the event loop picks them up.

**Healthy:** <10ms p99. **Warning:** >50ms (CPU-bound work or heavy sync I/O blocking the loop).
**Critical:** >100ms — the Node.js process is saturated.

Correlate spikes with GC pauses (major GC) or CPU usage.

panels.title: GC Duration Rate (seconds of GC per second)
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Time spent in garbage collection per second, by GC type.

**minor:** Young generation (scavenge) — frequent, fast, normal.
**major:** Old generation (mark-sweep-compact) — infrequent, slower, causes pauses.
**incremental:** Background marking — helps avoid long major pauses.

**Watch for:** Major GC dominating total GC time. If >10% of wall-clock time is spent in GC, the app is under severe memory pressure.

panels.title: Process CPU Usage (cores)
panels.description:
**What it measures:** CPU time consumed by the Node.js process, in CPU-cores.

A value of 1.0 = one full CPU core. Node.js is single-threaded for JS execution, so values approaching 1.0 mean the event loop is saturated.

**User CPU:** Time in application code.
**System CPU:** Time in kernel calls (I/O, networking).

panels.title: File Descriptors Used %
panels.description:
**What it measures:** Percentage of the maximum allowed file descriptors in use.

Each TCP connection, file handle, and pipe consumes a FD. Approaching the limit causes EMFILE errors and service failure.

title: Real Applications — Full Analytics
